results.title: Building a Whisper.cpp transcription app focused on ...
excerpts:
Section Title: Building a Whisper.cpp transcription app focused on accurate alignment — need thoughts
Content:
One thing worth mentioning is that the Groq setup is *practically free* for normal users right now. Since it’s user-owned usage with generous free limits, a regular person would struggle to hit those caps. That makes it attractive but only if accuracy holds up.
Right now the app already supports:
local whisper.cpp with CPU/GPU acceleration
WAV2VEC2 forced alignment
word-level timestamps
SRT, VTT, JSON exports
basic subtitle cue control
testing on English, Hindi, Hinglish
**Note:** This is an early prototype/testing UI. The current focus has been on transcription accuracy, forced alignment, and multilingual support. The final UI/UX will be significantly more polished and improved.
What I’m really trying to understand:

results.title: How Whisper AI Works: A Complete Guide | OpenWhispr
excerpts:
Section Title: How Whisper AI Works: A Complete Guide > Fact-Check Snapshot (Dual Sources)
Content:
[large-v2 model card](https://huggingface.co/openai/whisper-large-v2) · [benchmark details](https://github.com/openai/whisper/blob/main/model-card.md)
**Recent model updates:** large-v3 reports lower errors than large-v2 on many languages, and turbo focuses on lower latency with smaller decoder compute. [large-v3 model card](https://huggingface.co/openai/whisper-large-v3) · [turbo release discussion](https://github.com/openai/whisper/discussions/2363)
**OpenWhispr relevance:** OpenWhispr uses Whisper via whisper.cpp for local, offline-first dictation across desktop platforms. [whisper.cpp](https://github.com/ggml-org/whisper.cpp) · [OpenWhispr](https://openwhispr.com)
 ... 
Section Title: How Whisper AI Works: A Complete Guide > Fact-Check Snapshot (Dual Sources) > Model Sizes
Content:
Speed is relative to the large model. VRAM requirements are for GPU inference using fp16 precision via the original Python implementation. [whisper.cpp](https://github.com/ggml-org/whisper.cpp) uses significantly less memory (e.g., the large model requires ~3.9 GB RAM instead of ~10 GB VRAM).
The **turbo** model (released October 2024) is a distilled version of large-v3 with a significantly smaller decoder. It retains most of large-v3's accuracy while running approximately 8x faster — making it a strong choice when speed matters. Note that turbo does not support the translation task.
 ... 
Section Title: How Whisper AI Works: A Complete Guide > ... > Memory Requirements (whisper.cpp)
Content:
whisper.cpp runs on macOS (Intel and Apple Silicon), Linux, Windows, iOS, Android, FreeBSD, Raspberry Pi, and even in the browser via WebAssembly. It achieves faster-than-real-time transcription on modest consumer hardware — including devices as small as the Raspberry Pi 4 and iPhone 13.
This portability is what makes local, private speech recognition practical. Applications like [OpenWhispr](https://openwhispr.com) use whisper.cpp under the hood to provide real-time push-to-talk dictation that runs entirely on the user's device — no audio ever leaves the machine.

results.title: What Is whisper.cpp and Why You Might Want to Install It | by Dr. Shouke Wei | Jun, 2025 | Medium
excerpts:
Section Title: 🧠 What Is `whisper.cpp` ?
Content:
`[whisper.cpp](https://github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp)` is a **C++ implementation** of OpenAI’s [Whisper](https://openai.com/research/whisper) speech recognition model, optimized for **CPU inference** . It enables real-time or batch transcription **on-device** .

Section Title: whisper.cpp
Content:
Mac OS (Intel and Arm)
iOS
Android
Java
Linux / [FreeBSD](https://github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp/issues/56)
WebAssembly
Windows ( [MSVC](https://github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp/blob/master/.github/workflows/build.yml) and [MinGW](https://github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp/issues/168) ]
[Raspberry Pi](https://github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp/discussions/166)
[Docker](https://github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp/pkgs/container/whisper.cpp)
The entire high-level implementation of the model is contained in whisper.h and whisper.cpp .
The rest of the code is part of the [`ggml`](https://github.com/ggerganov/ggml) machine learning library.
Having such a lightweight implementation of the model allows to easily integrate it in different platforms and applications.
As an example, here is a video of running the model on an iPhone 13 device - fully offline, on-device: whisper.objc
https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/197385372-962a6dea-bca1-4d50-bf96-1d8c27b98c81.mp4
